content:
# Acts Chapter 8 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 234-235 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_008_page_001.jpg, ACT_008_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Saul stood in full agreement with Stephen's execution. That very day, a brutal wave of persecution crashed down on the church in Jerusalem, and all the believers -- except the apostles -- were scattered across the countryside of Judea and Samaria.

**2** Devout men carried Stephen to his burial, and their grief over him was loud and deep.

**3** But Saul was ravaging the church. He went house to house, dragging out men and women alike, and throwing them into prison.

**4** Now those who had been scattered went everywhere announcing the good news of the word.

**5** Philip went down to a city in Samaria and began proclaiming the Messiah to them.

**6** The crowds paid close attention -- with one mind -- to what Philip was saying, as they listened and watched the miraculous signs he was performing.

**7** For unclean spirits, shrieking at the top of their voices, came out of many who were possessed. And many who were paralyzed or lame were healed.

**8** So there was an explosion of joy in that city.

**9** Now a man named Simon had been practicing sorcery in the city for some time, astonishing the people of Samaria and claiming to be someone great.

**10** Everyone -- from the least to the greatest -- hung on his every word. "This man," they said, "is the Power of God, the one called Great."

**11** They had followed him for a long time because he had dazzled them with his magic arts.

**12** But when they believed Philip as he announced the good news about the kingdom of God and the name of Jesus Christ, both men and women were baptized.

**13** Even Simon himself believed. After his baptism, he attached himself to Philip and was amazed, watching the signs and extraordinary displays of power taking place.

**14** When the apostles in Jerusalem heard that Samaria had embraced the word of God, they sent Peter and John to them.

**15** When they arrived, they prayed for the new believers to receive the Holy Spirit,

**16** because the Spirit had not yet fallen on any of them -- they had simply been baptized into the name of the Lord Jesus.

**17** Then Peter and John laid their hands on them, and they received the Holy Spirit.

**18** When Simon saw that the Spirit was given through the laying on of the apostles' hands, he offered them money.

**19** "Give me this power too," he said, "so that anyone I lay my hands on will receive the Holy Spirit."

**20** But Peter said to him, "May your silver go to destruction with you, because you thought you could purchase the gift of God with money!

**21** You have no part or share in this work, because your heart is not right before God.

**22** Repent of this wickedness of yours and plead with the Lord -- perhaps the intent of your heart may be forgiven you.

**23** For I can see that you are poisoned with bitterness and shackled by sin."

**24** Simon answered, "Pray to the Lord for me yourselves, so that nothing you have said will happen to me."

**25** So after Peter and John had given their testimony and spoken the Lord's word, they headed back to Jerusalem, announcing the good news in many Samaritan villages along the way.

**26** Then an angel of the Lord said to Philip, "Get up and go south, to the desert road that runs from Jerusalem down to Gaza."

**27** So he got up and went. And there was an Ethiopian man, a eunuch and a court official of the Kandake -- the queen of the Ethiopians -- who was in charge of her entire treasury. He had come to Jerusalem to worship,

**28** and on his way home he was sitting in his chariot, reading the prophet Isaiah.

**29** The Spirit said to Philip, "Go over and stay close to that chariot."

**30** Philip ran up and heard him reading the prophet Isaiah aloud. "Do you understand what you are reading?" he asked.

**31** "How could I," the man replied, "unless someone guides me?" And he invited Philip to come up and sit beside him.

**32** The passage of Scripture he was reading was this:

*He was led like a sheep to the slaughter,*
*and like a lamb silent before its shearer,*
*he does not open his mouth.*

**33** *In his humiliation, justice was stripped from him.*
*Who will speak of his descendants?*
*For his life was taken from the earth.*

**34** The eunuch said to Philip, "Tell me, please -- who is the prophet talking about? Himself, or someone else?"

**35** Then Philip opened his mouth and, starting from that very passage of Scripture, told him the good news about Jesus.

**36** As they traveled along the road, they came to some water. "Look -- water!" said the eunuch. "What is to stop me from being baptized?"

**37** [This verse is not present in Codex Sinaiticus.]

**38** He ordered the chariot to stop, and both Philip and the eunuch went down into the water, and Philip baptized him.

**39** When they came up out of the water, the Spirit of the Lord swept Philip away. The eunuch did not see him again, but went on his way overflowing with joy.

**40** Philip, however, turned up at Azotus, and as he traveled through the region, he announced the good news in every town until he reached Caesarea.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 -- "crashed down":** The Greek (egeneto ... diogmos megas) conveys the sudden, violent onset of persecution. "Crashed down" captures the force and immediacy better than "arose" or "broke out."

**Verse 3 -- "ravaging":** The verb elumaineto carries the sense of destructive, brutal force -- like a wild animal mauling its prey. "Ravaging" preserves that visceral intensity.

**Verse 8 -- "explosion of joy":** The Greek chara megale ("great joy") in context follows miraculous healings and deliverances. "Explosion" conveys the communal, sudden, overwhelming quality of this joy, not merely "there was much happiness."

**Verse 10 -- "the Power of God, the one called Great":** Sinaiticus reads he dynamis tou theou he kaloumene megale. This is a title the Samaritans gave Simon. I preserved the grandiose phrasing to reflect how inflated his reputation was.

**Verse 20 -- "May your silver go to destruction with you":** Peter's words (to argurion sou sun soi eie eis apoleian) are a sharp, almost prophetic curse. "Go to destruction with you" captures the gravity without softening it into a mere wish.

**Verse 23 -- "poisoned with bitterness and shackled by sin":** The Greek eis cholen pikrias kai sundesmon adikias uses metaphors of gall/poison and bonds/chains. I rendered these as vivid English metaphors rather than transliterating the Greek idiom.

**Verse 33 -- "Who will speak of his descendants?":** The phrase ten genean autou tis diegesetai is notoriously difficult. In context (Isaiah 53:8 LXX), it may mean "who can describe his generation?" or "who will declare his posterity?" I chose "descendants" because the passage emphasizes the tragic cutting off of life -- no future generation to speak of.

**Verse 37 -- Absent in Sinaiticus:** Codex Sinaiticus does not contain verse 37 (the confession of faith before baptism), which is a later interpolation found in some Western manuscripts. I have noted its absence rather than supplying it.

**Verse 39 -- "swept Philip away":** The verb herpasen conveys sudden, forceful removal by the Spirit -- not a gentle departure but a dramatic snatching. "Swept away" captures both the divine agency and the startling nature of the event.

**Verse 39 -- "overflowing with joy":** The eunuch's joy (eporeueto chairon) is more than contentment. This man, excluded from full temple worship as a eunuch, has just been baptized into a community with no such barriers. His joy overflows.

-- PNEUMA
